promoted.title: Run Bugbot before you push
promoted.why: Move review left so the same agent catches bugs on the diff before the PR roundtrip starts.
how_to_steps: Connect the repository to Cursor and enable Bugbot for the repo in the dashboard.
Run /review or /review-bugbot locally before pushing so the agent inspects the exact diff first.
Turn on incremental review or mention-only mode to keep feedback focused on new commits.
Require the Cursor Bugbot check in branch protection once the team trusts the signal.
promoted.evidence: Cursor says Bugbot is over 3x faster, 22% cheaper, finds 10% more bugs per review, and 90% of runs now finish in under 3 minutes.
promoted.try_monday: Enable Bugbot on one active repo and require its check on draft PRs for one week.

promoted.title: Make GPT-5 mini the default coding workhorse
promoted.why: A cheap high-skill default model lets teams save the expensive model for cases where verification says more horsepower is actually needed.
how_to_steps: Set gpt-5-mini as the default model for routine implementation, repo Q and A, and PR-sized coding tasks.
Keep the loop verification-based so you escalate only when tests fail, tool use gets messy, or the task stalls.
Use lower verbosity for tight edit loops and increase it only when you need fuller plans or explanations.
Track solve rate and spend side by side for mini-default versus full-model escalations.
promoted.evidence: OpenAI reports GPT-5 mini scores 71.0% on SWE-bench Verified and 71.6% on Aider polyglot while costing $0.25 per 1M input tokens and $2 per 1M output tokens.
promoted.try_monday: Change one editor or agent default to GPT-5 mini and keep a simple escalation rule when verification fails.

promoted.title: Treat system-prompt edits like code changes
promoted.why: Prompt tweaks can silently degrade code quality, so they need the same gating discipline as production code.
how_to_steps: Run a broad per-model eval suite for every prompt change that affects an agent or coding workflow.
Use ablations to isolate which prompt line changes quality, latency, or token use.
Soak the change before full rollout if it trades off intelligence versus speed or cost.
Roll out gradually and revert immediately if the broader eval set regresses.
promoted.evidence: Anthropic reports that one verbosity instruction caused a 3% drop on both Opus 4.6 and 4.7 in a broader evaluation set, and it immediately reverted the change.
promoted.try_monday: Put your agent prompt under version control and block merges unless the change passes both narrow and broad eval suites.

promoted.title: Design containment at the environment layer first
promoted.why: Sandbox and egress boundaries keep failures bounded even when the model, a tool, or a user prompt goes off the rails.
how_to_steps: Choose the isolation pattern that matches the user and task: ephemeral container, workspace sandbox, or sealed VM.
Default to network deny and limit writes to the workspace or explicitly mounted paths.
Keep credentials outside the sandbox and pass only scoped session tokens inside.
Delay project-local config parsing until after trust is established, and inspect tool output before it reaches model context.
promoted.evidence: Anthropic says its Claude Code sandbox cut permission prompts by 84%, auto mode catches about 83% of overeager behaviors, and Gray Swan prompt-injection success is roughly 0.1% on single attempts.
promoted.try_monday: Move one internal coding agent into a workspace-only sandbox with network denied by default and audit what access it still truly needs.

promoted.title: Use always-on cloud agents for review, triage, and incident chores
promoted.why: Background automations keep review and maintenance work moving without waiting for someone to open the IDE.
how_to_steps: Create a new automation from the Agents Window, cursor.com/automations, or the /automate skill.
Pick a trigger such as pull request opened, CI completed, Slack, webhook, or cron.
Write the prompt and enable only the tools it needs, such as PR comments, Slack, MCP, or memories.
Choose whether it should run against one repo, multiple repos, or no repo, then save and activate it.
promoted.evidence: Cursor says Bugbot-like automations run thousands of times a day and have caught millions of bugs; its internal security-review automation has caught multiple vulnerabilities and critical bugs; Runlayer says the setup helps them move faster than teams five times their size.
promoted.try_monday: Ship one cron or PR-open automation that reviews merged code for missing tests and posts only high-confidence findings.

promoted.title: Index the repo so agents can search by meaning, not just strings
promoted.why: Semantic retrieval cuts the time an agent spends hunting for context and reduces follow-up correction loops.
how_to_steps: Open the workspace and let indexing begin automatically; semantic search becomes available at about 80% completion.
Check indexing status or trigger a rebuild from Cursor Settings > Indexing.
Add large generated paths to .cursorignore or .gitignore so the index stays focused on useful code.
Prompt the agent with behaviors like "where do we handle failed payments?" and let it combine semantic search with grep.
promoted.evidence: Cursor reports 12.5% higher codebase QA accuracy on average, 0.3% higher code retention overall, 2.6% higher retention on repos with 1,000+ files, and 2.2% more dissatisfied follow-ups when semantic search is unavailable.
promoted.try_monday: Re-index the main repo, trim noisy paths with .cursorignore, and compare agent answer quality on one thorny codebase question set.

promoted.title: Add Codex CLI as a terminal-first coding agent
promoted.why: A local terminal agent gives you long-running code work and PR review loops without being tied to a single editor.
how_to_steps: Install Codex CLI with the Mac or Linux install script, npm, Homebrew, or a release binary.
Run codex and sign in with ChatGPT so it uses the plan you already pay for.
Start code tasks from the terminal and choose an approval mode that matches your risk tolerance.
Escalate longer jobs to Codex cloud or GitHub review flows when you want work to keep running after you close the terminal.
promoted.evidence: OpenAI says GPT-5-Codex can work for more than 7 hours on complex tasks, cached cloud tasks cut median completion time by 90%, bottom-decile turns use 93.7% fewer tokens than GPT-5, and Codex reviews the vast majority of internal PRs while catching hundreds of issues per day.
promoted.try_monday: Install Codex CLI on one workstation and compare one feature branch done with terminal-agent help versus the current manual loop.

promoted.title: Cache stable repo context instead of resending it every turn
promoted.why: Repeated codebase context is expensive and slow; caching it turns long-running coding loops into faster, cheaper interactions.
how_to_steps: Put stable prompt material first, such as system policy, coding standards, tool schema, and repo summary.
Reuse that exact prefix across turns and agent tool loops, appending only the new task delta at the end.
Apply caching first to codebase Q and A, autocomplete, and multi-turn debugging sessions that resend long context.
Measure time to first token and input-token cost before and after the change.
promoted.evidence: Anthropic says prompt caching cuts cost by up to 90% and latency by up to 85%; one 100k-token prompt dropped from 11.5s to 2.4s time-to-first-token.
promoted.try_monday: Cache the stable repo and policy prefix in one high-volume coding assistant and compare TTFT and spend over a day.

promoted.title: Put a gateway in front of providers so outages do not stop the app
promoted.why: Routing through a gateway gives you model failover, provider order, and request-level visibility instead of a single brittle dependency.
how_to_steps: Route traffic through one gateway endpoint instead of wiring the app directly to a single provider SDK.
Set a primary model and a fallback models list in providerOptions.gateway.
Add provider ordering and fast timeouts so the gateway fails over quickly when a provider stalls.
Inspect modelAttempts and providerAttempts metadata, then monitor latency, spend, and failure patterns by route.
promoted.evidence: Vercel says AI Gateway is the same system that has powered v0 for millions of users, sits on infrastructure delivering sub-20ms latency, and removes the single-provider failure mode.
promoted.try_monday: Wrap one production AI endpoint with a primary model plus two fallbacks and log which model actually serves each request.

promoted.title: Graduate capability tests into an always-on regression suite
promoted.why: Once an agent can reliably do a task, keep that task in the regression bank so quality drift becomes visible immediately.
how_to_steps: Start with 20 to 50 real tasks drawn from bug reports, support tickets, or manual QA checks.
Split the bank into capability evals that are still hard and regression evals that should pass nearly 100% of the time.
Run tasks in a clean environment, use multiple trials where outputs vary, and prefer deterministic graders first.
When a capability task saturates, move it into the continuous regression suite and track latency, token use, and failure rate over time.
promoted.evidence: Anthropic recommends capability tasks that later graduate into a continuous regression suite and notes that fixing eval bugs plus scaffold issues moved one CORE-Bench result from 42% to 95%.
promoted.try_monday: Pick five agent tasks that already feel solved and convert them into a tiny must-pass regression suite.

discarded.title: Official GitHub MCP server
discarded.reason: Strong setup docs but not enough measured speed or robustness evidence for promoted jam.

discarded.title: How Anthropic teams use Claude Code
discarded.reason: Useful anecdotes, but the stronger jam came from the operational docs and postmortem-style sources.

discarded.title: Full GPT-5 as the default for every task
discarded.reason: The better weekly recommendation is cheap-by-default routing with explicit escalation rules.

notes: This run leans heavily on 2025-2026 official engineering write-ups and product docs because they include the strongest combination of how-to detail and measured outcomes.
The highest-value pattern across the week is verification and containment earlier in the workflow: pre-push review, eval-gated prompt changes, cheaper default routing, and failover-capable infra.
